student_name: Ana Paula Porto de Oliveira Fornazari
hub_subtitle: Inglês do dia a dia — apresentar-se, rotina e conversação

lesson.menu_title: The Way I Used To Live
lesson.menu_desc: Past habits and how everyday life changes -- used to for old routines and states, then and now, looking back on the way things were -- 34 slides
lesson.subtitle: Lesson 14 -- The Way I Used To Live
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Ana Paula Porto de Oliveira Fornazari | Lesson 14 -- The Way I Used To Live
lesson.grammar_point: used to for past habits and states

vocab.title: Match each word to its meaning
defs: well known to you and easy to recognize
in the end, after a long time
to feel sad that someone or something is not with you
the way a person lives from day to day
to think about something in your past
at that time in the past
to change a little so you feel comfortable in a new situation
at the present time, in contrast with the past
to suddenly understand or notice something
having warm, gentle feelings about someone or something
feeling happy and a little sad when you remember the past

reading.rtitle: The Way I Used To Live
paras: Back then, before I moved, my life was very different. I used to wake up to the sound of my neighbor's radio, and I used to walk to the same little bakery every morning. Everything felt familiar. I never used to worry about small things, because that was simply my routine.

Nowadays, my lifestyle is not the same at all. At first, everything felt strange, and I used to feel homesick almost every single day. I missed my family, my old street, and even the noisy traffic. But little by little, I started to adjust. I made new friends, I learned new roads, and eventually this new city started to feel like home too.

Sometimes I look back and feel a little nostalgic. A song on the radio reminds me of my old kitchen, and I smile. I have fond memories of both places now. I used to think that home was only one place, but I have realized that a person can belong to more than one.
reading.source: Alumni by Better -- A2+ reading, a learner remembers how her everyday life used to be

reading.prompt: What is the main idea of the text?
choices: Moving to a new country is a mistake and she wants to go back home.
Ana Paula remembers how her life used to be, and she has realized she can feel at home in more than one place.
It is a set of instructions for waking up early and walking to the bakery.

items: Before she moved, Ana Paula used to walk to the same bakery every morning.
The text says: 'I used to walk to the same little bakery every morning.'
When she first moved, Ana Paula felt at home right away.
The text says: 'everything felt strange, and I used to feel homesick almost every single day.'
A song on the radio reminds Ana Paula of her old kitchen.
The text says: 'A song on the radio reminds me of my old kitchen.'
Ana Paula still believes a person can belong to only one place.
The text says: 'I have realized that a person can belong to more than one.'

parts: my life is different, but I have started to
back on those days and feel

gapfill.text: We use 'used to' for a habit or a state that was true in the PAST but is not true now: I used to walk to the bakery (but I do not now). The form is always 'used to' + the base verb, for every subject: I / she / they used to live here. In the negative and the question, the 'd' disappears: I did not use to worry, Did you use to live there? Careful: 'used to' (past habit) is not the same as 'to be used to' from Lesson 13 (to be comfortable with something). Here we are talking about the past.

bank.label: Useful sentences to talk about the past and now
items: I used to live in a different city.
Back then, my daily routine was completely different.
At first I felt homesick, but eventually I adjusted.
This song reminds me of my old home.
I have fond memories of both places.
When I look back, I feel a little nostalgic.
I used to think home was only one place.

lf.title: Habit or state in the past?

lf.text: Notice the pattern: 'used to' + base verb works for both actions we repeated (used to walk, used to love) and states we were in (used to feel, used to think). It always means: true in the past, not true now. For the negative, we drop the 'd': I never used to worry / I did not use to worry.

items.situation: What is one thing you used to do as a child that you do not do now?
tips: As a child, I used to ...
I used to ..., but I do not anymore.

items.situation: How was your daily routine back then, before a big change in your life?
tips: Before I moved, my routine used to be ...

items.situation: What is one food or place you used to love? Do you still love it?
tips: I used to go to ... all the time.

items.situation: Think of a song or a smell that reminds you of the past. What memory does it bring back?
What is something you used to believe, but later realized was not true?
tips: I used to think ..., but then I realized ...
Back then I believed ..., but nowadays ...

items: Describe your old morning routine, before a big change in your life. Use 'used to' at least three times: 'I used to wake up ...', 'I used to ...'.
Compare back then and nowadays. Use the pattern 'I used to ..., but nowadays I ...' for three parts of your life.
Tell the whole story of a big change in your life, with no notes: how life used to be, how you felt at first, and how you eventually adjusted.
A friend asks: 'What do you miss most about the way things used to be, and what do you actually prefer nowadays?' Answer with a short story.

answerkey.title: Reveal the model answer
list: Model: 'Back then, I used to wake up early and walk to the same bakery every morning. My lifestyle used to be simple and familiar. When I first moved, I used to feel homesick, and I missed my old street. But little by little I started to adjust, and eventually the new city felt like home. Now I look back and feel a little nostalgic, but I have fond memories of both places.'
Past habits and states: used to + base verb (used to wake up, used to feel, used to be).
The 'd' disappears in the negative and question: I did not use to ... / Did you use to ...?
Remember the contrast with Lesson 13: 'used to do' (past habit) is different from 'to be used to doing' (to be comfortable with).
answerkey.note: Talking about how life used to be is one of the most natural things we do in real conversation. Use 'used to' for the old habit or state, and the present for how things are nowadays.

listenings.text: People who meet me now think I have always lived like this, but my life used to be completely different. Back then, I used to live in a small town where everybody knew my name. I used to walk everywhere, and I never used to lock my front door. When I moved to the city, everything felt strange. I used to get lost almost every day, and honestly, I used to feel very homesick. Little by little, though, I started to adjust. I found a coffee shop I liked, I learned the bus routes, and eventually the noise of the city started to feel normal. Nowadays, I actually love my busy lifestyle. But sometimes, when I look back, I feel a little nostalgic, and I have very fond memories of that quiet little town.

Hi Ana Paula, it is Sarah! I was cleaning out an old box today, and I found a photo of us from our first year of college. It reminded me of everything, and I got so nostalgic! Do you remember how we used to study together until midnight, and how we used to complain about every single exam? Back then, I used to think those were the hardest days of my life. Nowadays, I actually look back and miss them. My lifestyle is so different now; I used to have all the time in the world, and now my days are completely full. Anyway, it made me realize how much has changed. Tell me: what do you miss most about the way things used to be? Call me back when you can. Bye!

extra_audio.text: Back then, before I moved, my life used to be very simple. Every morning, I used to walk to the same little bakery. When I first arrived in the new city, I used to feel homesick and lost. Little by little, I started to adjust to my new lifestyle. Nowadays, I look back on those first days and feel a little nostalgic.
